learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Loops, Swapping, Time Complexity, Space Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine lockers numbered from 1 onwards. Some lockers contain the wrong keys, while some are empty. Your goal is to place every key into its correct locker. The first locker that remains empty tells you the smallest missing positive number.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine lockers numbered from 1 onwards. Some lockers contain the wrong keys, while some are empty. Your goal is to place every key into its correct locker. The first locker that remains empty tells you the smallest missing positive number.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Cyclic Sort (Index Placement) pattern whenever values belong to a fixed range and each value has a natural correct position inside the array.
learning.signals: Whenever numbers belong to a limited range and each number naturally maps to an index, think of Cyclic Sort (Index Placement).
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an unsorted integer array nums, return the smallest missing positive integer. You must implement an algorithm that runs in O(n) time and uses O(1) auxiliary space.

examples.explanation: 1 and 2 are present, so the smallest missing positive is 3.
After placing numbers correctly, 2 is missing.
No positive number starting from 1 exists.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Start from 1 and repeatedly search the array until the first missing positive is found.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Only positive numbers between 1 and n are useful because the answer always lies in the range [1, n + 1]. Place every valid number at its correct index (value x belongs at index x-1). After rearrangement, the first index where nums[i] != i + 1 gives the answer.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Each positive number has one unique correct index inside the array.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever numbers belong to a limited range and each number naturally maps to an index, think of Cyclic Sort (Index Placement).
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each element is swapped at most once into its correct position. Therefore, every element is processed only a constant number of times, resulting in O(n) time while using only constant extra space.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever numbers belong to a limited range and each number naturally maps to an index, think of Cyclic Sort (Index Placement).
commonWrongPaths: Not checking whether the value is within [1,n].
Infinite swapping due to duplicate values.
Returning the index instead of index+1.

hints: Ignore negative numbers and values larger than n.
Each valid number has one correct position.
commonMisconceptions: Not checking whether the value is within [1,n].
Infinite swapping due to duplicate values.
Returning the index instead of index+1.

pattern.name: Index Placement
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever numbers belong to a limited range and each number naturally maps to an index, think of Cyclic Sort (Index Placement).

approaches.title: Linear Search for Every Positive
approaches.idea: Start from 1 and repeatedly search the array until the first missing positive is found.

code.java: Brute force omitted because every search scans the entire array.

approaches.title: HashSet
approaches.idea: Insert every positive number into a HashSet, then search from 1 upward until a missing number is found.
algorithm: Store all positive numbers in a HashSet.
Return the first number not present.

approaches.title: Cyclic Sort (Index Placement)
approaches.idea: Traverse the array and place every valid positive integer x (1 ≤ x ≤ n) at index x-1 using swapping. After all numbers are placed, scan the array again. The first position where nums[i] != i+1 represents the smallest missing positive.
approaches.intuition: Each element is swapped at most once into its correct position. Therefore, every element is processed only a constant number of times, resulting in O(n) time while using only constant extra space.
algorithm: If nums[i] is in range [1,n] and not already in its correct position, swap it with nums[nums[i]-1].
Return the first index where nums[i] != i+1.
If every position is correct, return n+1.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[3,4,-1,1]
Swap 3↔-1 → [-1,4,3,1]
Swap 4↔1 → [-1,1,3,4]
Swap 1↔-1 → [1,-1,3,4]
Index1 should contain 2 but contains -1
Answer=2

visualization.description: Every value is placed into its own index.
steps: First incorrect position gives the answer.

revisionNotes: Swap valid numbers to correct indices.

interviewFollowUps: Can this be solved without modifying the array?
Why is the answer always between 1 and n+1?
How does this differ from Find All Missing Numbers?
Can negative marking solve this problem?
interviewTips: Explain why numbers outside [1,n] are ignored.
Mention that every swap places one number correctly.
Avoid infinite swaps by checking duplicates.
State O(n) time and O(1) space.
Mention HashSet as the simpler alternative.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Each positive number has one unique correct index inside the array.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Index Placement → Scan for First Mismatch.

importantPoints: Value x belongs at index x-1.
commonMistakes: Not checking whether the value is within [1,n].
Infinite swapping due to duplicate values.
Returning the index instead of index+1.

relatedProblems: Find All Numbers Disappeared in an Array
Find All Duplicates in an Array
